mappings.rationaleAI: The automobile is the central subject of C.4 (automobiles), connects to road vehicle development (A.5.a) and the wheel-and-road history (A.2), covering propulsion, suspension, braking, safety systems, and the automotive industry's evolution.

Vehicle covers all transport modes — wheeled, tracked, railed, water, air, space — providing the broadest conceptual framework for section C (road vehicles), F.1 (waterborne vessels), and G.1 (aircraft) simultaneously.

Covers the full history and typology of transportation across all modes — road, rail, water, air, pipeline — providing a broad foundation for the history of transportation (A) and connecting all sub-sections of 734.

Civil engineering encompasses road and highway construction (B) as a core sub-discipline, and harbour and coastal engineering (F.4) covering docks and quays; it also includes transportation engineering as a named sub-discipline.

Directly covers rail transportation (D) in full — from early steam locomotives through diesel and electric traction to high-speed rail and maglev — and the historical spread of railways (A.4.a) as a defining phase of steam transportation.

Directly covers canals and inland waterways (F.3) — navigation canals with locks, summit canals, and the Panama Canal as exemplar — including their historical role in Industrial Revolution bulk freight transport.

Directly covers the aircraft classification (G.1) — lighter-than-air (G.1.a: airships, balloons) and heavier-than-air (G.1.b: fixed-wing, rotorcraft) — with propulsion and flight characteristics essential to the air transport section.

The Industrial Revolution is the defining era of steam transportation (A.4), covering the first locomotives and spread of railways (A.4.a) and early steamships and iron vessels (A.4.b) that revolutionized both land and water transport.

The ICE is the dominant power unit for automobiles (C.4) and marine propulsion (F.2.c), covering Otto and diesel cycles, gas turbines, and jet engines — all directly listed as power units for propulsion in F.2.c.

Directly covers types of ships (F.1) — bulk carriers, tankers, container ships — and ship design and construction (F.2), including hull form, propulsion systems, and cargo architecture across the global merchant fleet.

Engines as energy converters underpin automobile propulsion (C.4), ship power units including steam, diesel, and gas turbines (F.2.c), and aircraft propulsion (G.1.b), making the article relevant across road, water, and air transport modes.

The steam engine is the definitive technology of the railroad era (A.4.a) and steam navigation (A.4.b), and steam-powered locomotives drove the development of rail transportation (D) throughout the 19th century.

Directly covers bicycles (C.3) — the mechanical systems, dynamics, and historical development of human-powered two-wheeled transport — as the primary and only substantive candidate for that priority path.

Directly covers roads and highways (B) — types, features, pavement maintenance, and engineering — and the history of road development from the ancient world (A.2), connecting to wheel-based transportation history.

Radar is a foundational technology in aeronautical research and navigation systems (G.5), used in air traffic control, autonomous vehicle guidance, and meteorology; it bridges military origins with civilian aviation applications.

Ford's development of mass automobile production through the moving assembly line is central to the construction of road vehicles (A.5.a) and the evolution of the automobile (C.4), making him the defining industrialist for those paths.

The wheel is the foundational invention for road-based transportation (A.2) and the defining component of all wheeled vehicles in section C, covering the mechanics of rolling contact and the development of vehicle wheels from antiquity.

Rockets are the primary propulsion technology for space travel (G.4) and the subject of ongoing aeronautical and space research (G.5), covering propellant types, multistage designs, and the trajectory from military origins to satellite launches and crewed spaceflight.

Bridge construction is an integral part of road and highway infrastructure (B, A.5.a), as the outline explicitly notes 'construction of railroad bridges and tunnels' under steam transportation history; bridges are essential infrastructure for all surface transport modes.

Navigation covers the methods and instruments for controlling vehicle movement across marine (F) and aeronautical (G) domains, spanning celestial navigation, radar, and satellite GNSS systems essential to both water and air transportation.

Electric motors are the propulsion technology for electric vehicles (C.4), electric rail traction (D), and ship propulsion systems (F.2.c), and their regenerative operation as generators is central to modern hybrid and battery-electric transport.

Provides the broad historical narrative of transportation technology development — from animal power through steam to modern internal combustion and digital systems — directly supporting the history of transportation section (A).

Infrastructure encompasses the physical transport networks — roads, railways, energy grids — that constitute the built substrate of transportation, directly relating to roads and highways (B) and rail infrastructure (D) as core components of transport systems.

Covers ocean and sea environments — currents, tides, maritime navigation, trade, and fisheries — providing the physical and historical context for water transportation (F) including the development of shipping and maritime infrastructure.

Modern avionics, navigation systems, and flight control rely on electronics — transistors, integrated circuits, and semiconductor devices — making this a relevant contributor to aeronautical and space research (G.5), though the overlap is indirect.
